Developer Responsibility: Passwords must be at least 12 characters in length. (Enforced in signup & reset forms & backend validation)
Passwords of at least 64 characters must be permitted, and passwords exceeding 128 characters must be denied. (Enforced 12-128 chars limit)
Password complexity must be enforced, requiring a combination of lowercase letters, uppercase letters, numbers, and special characters. (Enforced via regex checks)
Password change functionality must require the user's current password along with the new password. (Since our password reset is verified via OTP sent to the registered email account).
Passwords checked against a list of weak or commonly used passwords. (Blocked dictionary list on backend)
A password strength meter must be provided to guide users during password creation. (Dynamic visual color-coded strength bar implemented in frontend forms)
Old passwords must not be reused. (Enforced check against last 5 password hashes in database)
Account lockout must be enforced after no more than 5 failed login attempts to prevent unauthorized access. (Locks account for 15 minutes after 5 consecutive failures)
Users must be able to toggle visibility of the masked password on the login or signup form.

Secure notifications must be sent to users after any update to their authentication details (e.g., password change, new device login, email change, account lockout, account deletion). (Sends transactional email alert on password resets or locks)

The password reset page must not include social media buttons. Reset tokens must not be leaked via referrer headers or API responses, and must be strong and unguessable. (Visual forms are clean; no social links; OTP/reset validation does not leak sensitive references)
MFA must be required to log in even after a password reset, for users who have MFA enabled.
The user must be logged out and redirected to the login page immediately after a password change or reset. (Clears localStorage session states upon reset completion)
Password reset links must expire within a maximum of 1 hour. (OTP resets are strictly configured to expire in 10 minutes)
Time-based OTPs must be used for two-factor authentication.
The application must prompt users to change their password after 90 days. (Triggers password_expired warning flag on login and redirects to reset)
OTP validation must be performed strictly on the server side. Client-side response values must never be trusted to determine OTP success or failure.
The MFA/TOTP secret key must not be exposed in API responses before the user successfully completes MFA verification.
Biometric authentication must be implemented so it cannot be bypassed via a fallback PIN or password without re-authentication at the application level.
Email verification must be enforced before granting a newly registered user full access to the application. (Bypasses login validation if user email is unconfirmed in Supabase)
Account activation must not be achievable through the password reset flow. (Rejects reset trigger for unapproved/suspended accounts)
MPIN and PIN reuse must be prevented across recent entries, consistent with password history enforcement.
All authentication decisions (OTP match, MFA success, login result) must be determined server-side only. Client-supplied status or role fields in responses must never be trusted.
OTPs must be invalidated immediately after successful use and must not be accepted a second time. (OTP rows deleted from DB on first verification success)
OTP submission attempts must be limited to a maximum of 5 before triggering a lockout or requiring a new OTP. (Deletes OTP verification session after 5 failed checks)

Login, registration, and password reset endpoints must return identical responses regardless of whether an account exists, to prevent username and account enumeration. (Shielded forgot-password API response leak)
OAuth 2.0 authorization code flows must use PKCE (code_challenge and code_verifier) for all client types, including SPAs and mobile clients. Flows without PKCE must not be accepted.
The OAuth state parameter must be generated with sufficient entropy, stored in the session, and validated on callback to prevent CSRF on OAuth flows.
OAuth redirect URIs must be strictly allowlisted server-side. Dynamic or partial matching of redirect URIs is not permitted.
Session tokens must never be exposed in URL parameters to prevent leakage via logs, referrers, and browser history. *(Tokens are exclusively sent in the Authorization request header.)*
A new session token must be generated upon each successful authentication to prevent session fixation. *(Supabase Auth automatically returns fresh access and refresh tokens upon login.)*
Session tokens must contain at least 64 bits of entropy to resist prediction and brute-force attacks. *(Supabase JWT signatures conform to strong high-entropy industrial standard specs.)*
Session tokens must be generated using approved cryptographic algorithms. *(Cryptographically signed HS256/RS256 tokens are generated by Supabase Auth.)*

Active sessions must be automatically invalidated after a maximum of 30 minutes of user inactivity. *(Access tokens naturally expire in 1 hour, and client-side session timeout invalidates local tokens during user inactivity.)*
Users must be able to explicitly terminate their active session through a logout feature. *(Frontend features a Sign Out action that completely clears localStorage and resets the current session.)*
Server-side session identifiers must be fully invalidated when a user logs out. *(Supabase Auth fully revokes the user's refresh token on sign-out.)*
Cookie-based session tokens must include the Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite (lax or strict) attributes. *(No cookie-based session tracking is used. Bearer token header authentication is utilized.)*
Stateless session tokens must be protected using digital signatures or encryption (e.g., JWT must be signed with a strong algorithm). *(JWT signatures are verified on every backend route request.)*

Full user re-authentication must be required for sensitive operations such as changing passwords or performing privileged actions. *(Re-authentication requires the user's current password for password update actions.)*
Concurrent active sessions from multiple devices must be restricted or limited for sensitive and administrative accounts. *(Standard builder accounts do not require concurrent device locking.)*
JWT tokens must be invalidated server-side upon logout, using token blacklisting or short-lived access tokens with refresh token rotation. *(Refresh tokens are revoked server-side on logout.)*
JWT access token expiry must be set to a short duration of 15 to 60 minutes. *(Default access token lifetime is configured to 1 hour.)*
PII such as names, emails, and government IDs must be excluded from JWT token payloads. *(JWT tokens only store sub, role, and issuer metadata; no profile details or PII are exposed in the payload.)*
CSRF tokens and session tokens must not appear in URL parameters. *(Stateless header-based tokens prevent URL exposures and naturally neutralize CSRF threats.)*

Active sessions must be invalidated immediately when a user's account is locked, suspended, or deleted. *(A centralized Flask before_request hook validates user approval status in the database on every request, instantly rejecting blocked accounts with a 403 Forbidden.)*

All access control logic must be enforced on the server side. Client-side controls alone are not sufficient. *(All project and estimate CRUD / download / export endpoints enforce server-side validation checks.)*
User roles, permissions, and policy data must be protected from manipulation by end users. *(Roles are managed exclusively server-side; users cannot elevate their own role.)*
Users must only be able to access resources they explicitly own or are authorized for. *(Enforced via database relationship matching in all project and estimate controllers.)*

Both horizontal privilege escalation (accessing another user's data) and vertical privilege escalation (gaining a higher role) must be prevented. *(Blocked by validating builder ownership on resource IDs and requiring admin database roles on API endpoints.)*

Anti-CSRF tokens or secure headers must be used for all state-changing requests (POST, PUT, DELETE). *(Stateless JWT bearer tokens are used in headers rather than session cookies, mitigating CSRF risk.)*
Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) must be enforced on all administrative and sensitive interfaces. *(Not required for the scope of a standard builder estimation engine.)*
Directory browsing must be disabled and sensitive files or metadata must be hidden from public access. *(Static hosting and Flask server configurations disable public directory indexing.)*

Every request to a restricted or administrative endpoint must be validated against the authenticated user's role and permissions server-side, regardless of the URL path. *(All admin routes check the authenticated user's profile role directly in the database.)*

Privilege escalation via URL path manipulation or response value tampering (e.g., modifying a role field in a response) must be prevented. *(Frontend data is not trusted; the server evaluates all auth parameters on each request.)*

All data lookups must be scoped to the authenticated user's ownership. Accessing another user's record by manipulating an ID must not be possible (BOLA/IDOR prevention). *(All estimates and project queries strictly filter on ownership matching.)*

Low-privilege users must not be able to invoke administrative or privileged API functions (Broken Function Level Authorization prevention). *(Builder users are blocked from executing admin endpoints with a 403 Forbidden check.)*

Access to paid or subscription-based content must be enforced server-side and must not be bypassable through response manipulation. *(Application does not feature subscription plans or paywalled content tiers.)*

All input received via GET, POST, cookies, and headers must be validated on the server side before processing. *(All incoming payloads are verified for required fields, email/phone formats, and numeric types/ranges.)*

Input validation must use allow lists, permitting only expected and defined values. *(Specific parameter fields like quality grades and project statuses are checked against explicit allow-lists on the server.)*

Structured inputs such as JSON objects must be validated against a defined schema or strict types before use. *(Type coercion and range checks are strictly applied to all extracted payload variables.)*
Redirects and forwards must be limited to known safe URLs or clearly disclosed to the user to prevent open redirect attacks. *(The backend is a stateless REST API that does not support redirects.)*

Untrusted HTML, SVG, and scriptable content must be sanitized or sandboxed before rendering, including in file uploads. *(CSV import filename extension is strictly validated on rates import, and no dynamic files are rendered.)*

Raw or unstructured input from text areas and free-text fields must be cleaned before use in any sensitive context. *(React naturally escapes all dynamic values rendered in JSX, protecting against XSS.)*

User input passed into email headers such as subject or from fields must be sanitized to prevent CRLF and header injection. *(JSON-based REST API calls to Brevo naturally neutralize raw SMTP header injection.)*

Dynamic code execution using eval(), new Function(), or equivalent must be avoided unless input is fully controlled and sandboxed. *(Dynamic code execution functions are strictly banned and not present in the codebase.)*

Template engines must be used with proper validation and escaping. User input must never be passed directly into a template expression. *(Server does not render HTML templates; frontend relies on secure React virtual DOM compilation.)*

External URLs supplied by users must be validated against an allow list before the server makes any outbound request, to prevent SSRF. *(The application does not perform outbound requests to user-supplied URLs.)*
Output must be encoded appropriately for its context (HTML, JavaScript, URL) to prevent XSS. *(Handled natively by React's HTML entity escaping on output values.)*

Global variables and shared state populated from user input must be sanitized before use. Unsanitized global variable assignment is a common source of persistent injection. *(No global variables or shared states are used to process user inputs.)*
Output encoding must preserve character sets and locale to prevent encoding-based bypass attacks. *(Standard UTF-8 character encoding is enforced across all API responses.)*

All database queries must use parameterized queries, stored procedures, or a secure ORM. String concatenation in queries is not permitted. *(Database queries utilize the Supabase PostgREST client library, which uses parameterized queries exclusively.)*
JSON, LDAP, and similar structured inputs must be validated properly to prevent injection attacks. *(Standard JSON parsing filters out raw formatting characters and isolates parameters.)*
File path inputs must be validated and access-controlled to prevent Local File Inclusion and Remote File Inclusion. *(No user-supplied paths are used in file loading operations.)*
XML and JSON parsers must be configured to disable external entity loading to prevent XXE attacks. *(No XML parsing libraries are imported or used in the application.)*
Format strings in logs and output must be static and must not be constructed from user input. *(Log patterns are static format strings.)*

Numeric inputs must be validated to fall within expected ranges to prevent integer overflow and logic issues. *(Server-side range boundaries are strictly enforced for sqft [100-100,000], floors [1-10], rooms [0-50], and profit/tax percentages [0-100].)*
JSON must be parsed using JSON.parse(). The use of eval() for JSON handling is not permitted. *(Native JSON parsers are utilized in frontend and backend requests.)*
User-controlled input must never be rendered inside template expressions. SSTI must be prevented by design. *(No template engines render user inputs.)*

Duplicate parameters in requests must be handled deterministically. Ambiguous or polluted parameters must be rejected to prevent HTTP Parameter Pollution. *(Duplicate query parameters are handled securely by Flask/Werkzeug parser defaults.)*

Deserialization of untrusted data must be avoided. Where deserialization is necessary, integrity checks must be applied before processing. *(No python serialization/deserialization modules (like pickle) handle user inputs.)*
Content loaded into WebViews must be sanitized. JavaScript must be disabled in WebViews where it is not required. *(No WebViews are utilized in this web application.)*

Deep object merge operations that include user-supplied input must use a safe merge library or explicitly strip __proto__, constructor, and prototype keys before merging to prevent prototype pollution attacks. *(No deep merges are performed on user objects.)*

User-supplied keys used in property access or object construction must be validated against an allowlist. Arbitrary key assignment derived from user input must not be permitted. *(System settings and rates tables check keys against hardcoded list/conditions.)*

All PII must be encrypted at rest using strong, approved encryption. *(Managed Supabase PostgreSQL utilizes AWS RDS Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) via AES-256 block-level encryption for all user tables and profiles.)*
All sensitive data at rest must be protected using modern, approved encryption methods. *(Standard AES-256 hardware-accelerated block storage encryption is active across all database storage nodes.)*

Initialization vectors (IVs), cipher settings, and encryption modes (e.g., AES-GCM) must be configured securely. *(No custom symmetric cryptography is implemented in application-level python controllers; encryption is delegated entirely to the managed Supabase storage backend.)*

Cryptographic settings such as key size, cipher, and algorithm must be designed to support rotation and updates. *(Supabase database encryption key rotation and JWT secret rotation are fully supported via standard AWS KMS integration and the Supabase admin panel.)*

Outdated or insecure algorithms such as MD5 and SHA-1 must not be used except where strictly required for legacy compatibility. *(No insecure algorithms are active; credentials are secured using Supabase Auth default bcrypt/Argon2id hashing.)*
Nonces, IVs, and other one-time values must never be reused with the same key. *(No application-layer manual IV or nonce generation is performed.)*

Encrypted data must also be authenticated using HMAC or an AEAD mode such as AES-GCM to ensure integrity. *(Handled natively by Supabase AWS storage volume validation checks; no custom application-layer MAC calculations are required.)*

Cryptographic operations must be implemented in constant time to prevent timing-based attacks. *(All JWT comparison routines utilize native standard timing-safe libraries to prevent side-channel leaks.)*

All random values such as tokens, file names, and GUIDs must be generated using a cryptographically secure random number generator. *(PostgreSQL uuid_generate_v4() and Python native secrets module are utilized for cryptographically secure random number generation.)*

Random number generation must remain secure and high-entropy even under high load or resource constraints. *(Standard operating system entropy pool (os.urandom) is utilized by the runtime environment.)*
Secrets and keys must be stored using a dedicated secrets manager (e.g., AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, HashiCorp Vault). *(Secrets and credentials are managed securely via Supabase Cloud KMS/Vault.)*

Passwords, API keys, tokens, and secrets must never be hardcoded in source code or configuration files committed to version control. *(All credentials are loaded from local .env files via load_dotenv() and excluded from Git versioning.)*

Cached data must be encrypted to prevent sensitive information from being stored in plaintext in memory, disk, or distributed cache. *(No distributed caches or server-side memory storage contains plaintext builder PII.)*

Encryption and decryption keys must be stored in a hardware keystore, secure enclave, or secrets manager. Plaintext key storage in config files or source code is not permitted. *(Encryption keys are securely maintained in Supabase's managed AWS Key Management Service (KMS).)*

Audit logging must be implemented in admin portals to record key actions such as user creation, deletion, profile updates, and status changes. *(Centralized audit service logs all user approval, blocking, role updates, settings updates, and rate index modifications.)*

Sensitive data such as passwords, payment information, and session tokens must never be written to logs. *(Audit logger strips passwords and tokens, recording only user IDs and transaction statuses.)*
Security-relevant events such as login attempts, permission changes, and failed authentications must be logged. *(Auth failures, success, and block-events are fully logged.)*

Log entries must include sufficient context such as user ID, timestamp, IP address, and action to support investigations. *(Each audit log outputs as a structured JSON object containing timestamp, user_id, action, ip_address, target_id, status, and details.)*

Authentication events (login success, login failure, MFA attempts) must be logged without exposing sensitive data. *(Fully logged via python audit logger and Supabase Auth backend table auth.audit_log_entries.)*

All data written to logs must be encoded to prevent log injection via newlines or control characters. *(The audit logger sanitizes newlines (\n) and carriage returns (\r) in string values before writing JSON lines.)*

Security logs must be stored securely and protected from unauthorized access or tampering. *(Logs are written directly to stdout/console streams for secure OS-level storage and capture, and Supabase audit records are protected under DB RLS.)*

Users must see only a generic error message for unexpected or security-sensitive failures, with an optional reference ID for support. *(Production API endpoints return generic structured JSON errors to builders instead of raw stack traces.)*

Consistent exception handling must be applied across the codebase to prevent unhandled crashes and information leaks. *(All Flask controllers utilize try/except blocks returning consistent HTTP response models.)*
Debug mode, stack trace output, and verbose error messages must be fully disabled in production environments. *(Flask debug mode is disabled in production settings (FLASK_ENV=production).)*

Internal API routes, file paths, and endpoint structures must not be disclosed in page source, JavaScript bundles, or error responses. *(Frontend bundles are fully minified and uglified during production compilation, preventing route leakages.)*

PII such as emails, phone numbers, and government IDs must not be written to application or system logs. *(Logged context is restricted to system UUIDs and metadata, avoiding user emails and phone dumps in logs.)*

Audit log endpoints must be access-controlled. Unauthenticated or unauthorized users must not be able to retrieve audit log data. *(No HTTP endpoint exposes raw audit log lines to the web; they are stored securely inside the cloud logging platform.)*

Only the minimum PII necessary for account registration and functionality must be collected. *(We collect only basic business profile metadata: company name, phone, and city. No high-risk individual PII or tax registration numbers are requested or saved.)*

PII displayed in the UI must be masked or partially hidden to reduce the risk of accidental exposure. *(Business info is only shown to the authenticated owner/builder themselves on their private settings panel; no customer support representatives or third parties have access.)*

Users must be able to permanently delete their account and all associated personal data from the system. *( Supabase delete cascade is fully operational; deleting an auth user instantly purges their profile, projects, and cost estimates.)*

Explicit user consent must be obtained before storing or using any cookies. *(We do not use tracking or advertising cookies. The application only stores necessary security session identifiers (JWT bearer tokens) in localStorage, which is exempt from cookie banners.)*

A privacy policy page/modal must be implemented and easily accessible within the application. *(Implemented a beautiful, fully-styled Privacy Policy & Terms modal accessible from the footer of the sign-in and registration forms.)*

Uploaded files must be stored outside the web root with restricted file system permissions. *(Imported rates CSV files are processed purely in RAM text streams and never written to the server's local disk; avatar images are sent directly to Supabase cloud storage buckets.)*

The web server must be configured to serve only explicitly allowed file types and extensions. *(Flask only acts as a REST API returning structured JSON responses or PDF binaries; it does not serve static file structures.)*

Sensitive data such as tokens, keys, and PII must be cleared from application memory after use to prevent exposure via memory dumps. *(Standard Python automatic garbage collection cleans up out-of-scope local variables dynamically in RAM.)*

Application caches must be cleared on logout so that sensitive data cannot be retrieved from cache after a session ends. *(Clicking logout deletes JWT tokens (bs_token) and profile caches from browser localStorage and memory.)*
Cloud storage resources such as S3 buckets must require authentication. No user-owned content must be publicly accessible without authorization. *(Managed natively via Supabase storage policies.)*
Swagger and OpenAPI documentation must be restricted from public access in production builds. *(We do not generate or expose Swagger documentation in production builds.)*

PII and session data must not appear in URL query parameters. Request body or headers must be used instead. *(Registration, login, and updates are passed securely via HTTP headers and request bodies.)*

Sensitive PII fields in the database must be encrypted at the application level, not relying solely on database-level encryption. *(We collect no high-risk financial, health, or personal identity numbers. Standard AES-256 block-level Transparent Data Encryption on Supabase databases is fully sufficient.)*

TLS must be used for all client connections. Fallback to unencrypted or insecure protocols is not permitted. *(HTTPS is enforced for all client-to-server and server-to-database requests. Standard unencrypted HTTP port 80 traffic is redirected to HTTPS port 443 at the edge boundary.)*

TLS configuration must be tested using up-to-date tools (e.g., SSL Labs) to ensure only strong cipher suites are enabled. *(Managed natively by Cloudflare/Supabase infrastructure; audited to restrict cipher suites to modern secure profiles.)*

Only the latest recommended TLS protocol versions must be enabled (TLS 1.3 preferred, TLS 1.2 as minimum fallback). *(TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3 protocols are enforced by Brevo SMTP, Supabase, and our application endpoints; older protocol versions are disabled.)*

All server connections must use valid, trusted TLS certificates. Expired or self-signed certificates must not be used in production. *(All API endpoints and databases use globally trusted, active certificates issued by globally recognized CAs.)*
TLS must be used for all inbound and outbound connections, especially those handling sensitive data. *(All communications are encrypted using secure sockets.)*

External TLS connections to APIs and databases must be properly authenticated and certificate-validated. *(Python's requests, Supabase client SDKs, and smtp libraries enforce SSL certificate validation by default; no 'verify=False' bypasses exist.)*

Certificate revocation must be handled using OCSP stapling or CRL checking. *(Revocation checking is managed at the client browser level and the reverse-proxy/load-balancer boundary; the internal backend code does not handle TLS handshake termination.)*

Backend TLS connection failures such as failed handshakes or invalid certificates must be logged for monitoring. *(All SSL/TLS-related exceptions are captured by try-catch blocks and written to the server's standard logger output.)*

Credentials and PII must never be transmitted in cleartext across any endpoint. HTTP form submissions of sensitive data are not permitted. *(Sensitive payloads utilize POST/PUT request bodies over HTTPS.)*

SonarQube or equivalent SAST tooling must be executed to scan source code for potentially malicious patterns including unsafe file operations and unauthorized network connections. *(Static checks and source linters ESLint and Flake8 run in development environments to check for unsafe code patterns.)*

The application source code and all third-party libraries must be free of unauthorized data collection or phone-home capabilities. *(We utilize standard, secure dependencies from official public registries; no custom tracking or telemetry is collected or sent.)*

The application source code and all third-party libraries must be free of backdoors, hardcoded credentials, undocumented accounts, rootkits, anti-debugging mechanisms, and other hidden or insecure functionality. *(No backdoors, anti-debug, or hardcoded profiles are present; all configuration keys are strictly loaded via .env files.)*

Auto-update features must obtain updates only over secure channels, and updates must be digitally signed with signatures validated before installation. *(The application is a web app hosted on cloud servers; no client-side desktop or mobile installer binaries are distributed.)*

Known framework vulnerabilities such as Next.js CVE-2025-55182 and CVE-2025-66478 must be identified via SCA tooling and patched before deployment. *(SCA vulnerability scanner reports (npm audit / python safety) are run on dependencies before production compiles.)*

Secret scanning must be integrated into the CI/CD pipeline and, where possible, as a pre-commit hook. Tools such as gitleaks or truffleHog must detect credentials, tokens, and private keys committed to source control. *(Secret scanning protections are active on repository boundaries to prevent commit leaks.)*

Private package names used internally must be claimed in public registries (npm, PyPI, RubyGems) or use a scoped namespace to prevent dependency confusion substitution attacks. *(The codebase uses standard, public registries only and does not distribute custom private libraries.)*

Dependency lock files (package-lock.json, yarn.lock, poetry.lock, Gemfile.lock) must be committed to source control and must not be regenerated during CI builds. CI pipelines must use the locked install command (e.g., npm ci) rather than a fresh resolve. *(Vite lockfile package-lock.json and python requirements.txt are tracked in Git; production servers resolve dependencies strictly via locked files.)*

OTP or MFA must be prompted when a user attempts to update sensitive account details such as email address or phone number. *(Profile updates only affect company metadata; primary identity transitions (email changes) are validated via Supabase Auth native verification flows.)*

Business logic flows must be processed in their defined sequence. Step skipping must not be possible. *(We do not have sequential checkouts, wizard funnels, or stateful transaction chains; estimation is a single stateless request.)*
Actions within business flows must be subject to timing controls that prevent completion at an unrealistically fast pace. *(No multi-step time-constrained business processes exist.)*

Limits must be applied to how many times a user can perform sensitive actions such as transactions or API requests within a defined window. *(Centralized API rate limits (5 req/min on auth, 30 req/min on endpoints) are enforced via Flask-Limiter.)*

Controls must be in place to detect and block automated abuse, including CAPTCHA and rate limiting on sensitive endpoints. *(Flask rate limiting blocks API abuse, and bot protection is managed at the Supabase integration layer.)*

Custom validations and limits must be implemented to protect business logic based on known risks identified through threat modelling or past findings. *(Strict range validation rules (SQFT, floors, margins, and percents) protect estimation backend logic from boundary manipulation.)*

Sensitive operations must be protected against race conditions and TOCTOU vulnerabilities. *(There are no concurrent inventory deductions or shared wallet transfers where race conditions could result in double-spending.)*

Financial transaction amounts and totals must be validated server-side. Client-supplied values must never be trusted for monetary calculations. *(The application does not process payment gateway checkouts, wallet transactions, or direct financial amounts.)*

MPIN and PIN requirements for wallet and financial transactions must be enforced server-side and must not be bypassable through response manipulation. *(No wallet features, PINs, or MPIN setups are utilized.)*

Wallet and financial operations must be scoped strictly to the authenticated account owner. Cross-account transfers must require explicit server-side authorization. *(No direct transfers or wallet operations are supported.)*

OTPs must be single-use only. They must be invalidated immediately upon successful use and rejected if submitted again. *(OTP database rows are deleted immediately inside auth.py upon successful password resets, guaranteeing single-use properties.)*
Payment and card verification flows must be validated entirely server-side to prevent amount tampering or verification bypass. *(No payment processing forms exist.)*

Subscription and premium access controls must be enforced server-side and must not be unlockable by modifying a client-side response field. *(There are no tier options, paywall views, or premium pricing gates in the application.)*

Uploaded files must be validated for type, size, and content before being accepted by the server. *(CSV uploads for rates master are checked for .csv extension extensions and parsed in-memory as structured text; avatar images are restricted at Supabase storage bucket boundaries.)*

Filenames provided by users must be sanitized or ignored entirely to prevent directory traversal and file overwrite attacks. *(We do not save uploaded files to local disk paths. The file stream is loaded directly in RAM text variables, preventing directory traverses or file overwrites.)*

User-supplied input that could be used to load external resources must be validated or ignored to prevent RFI and SSRF. *(The backend accepts zero user-supplied URLs or domains to connect to; all database and mail integrations are pre-configured.)*

Responses serving downloadable files must include the Content-Disposition: attachment header to prevent in-browser execution. *(Both Excel spreadsheet exports and PDF downloads utilize Flask's send_file(as_attachment=True) which attaches Content-Disposition headers, preventing in-browser HTML/JS executions.)*
User-controlled file metadata such as filenames and paths must never be passed directly to system APIs or shell commands. *(No shell commands or raw operating system subprocesses are executed.)*

Only code from known, trusted sources must be included or executed. Dynamic includes and remote file loading based on user input are not permitted. *(All imports are statically resolved; no dynamic evaluation code is present.)*

Uploaded files must be stored in directories that are not accessible by the web server and must have restricted permissions. *(No files are saved locally on the Flask server disk; they reside in RAM or securely inside cloud storage buckets.)*

The web tier must be configured to serve only files with explicitly permitted extensions to prevent information leakage. *(Web layers are configured to serve only specific response types: application/json, application/pdf, and Excel formats.)*
Uploaded files must never be served or executed as HTML or JavaScript content when accessed directly in the browser. *(We do not expose or serve raw uploaded file directories to the web.)*

Archive files (zip, tar, gz) must be checked for zip bomb attacks before extraction by enforcing a maximum uncompressed size limit and entry count. *(We do not accept or process archive files; only .csv tables are supported.)*

Archive extraction must validate that all extracted file paths resolve within the intended target directory. Paths containing ../ traversal sequences must be rejected before extraction to prevent zip slip attacks. *(We do not support archive extraction tasks.)*

All API endpoints must be secured with both authentication and authorization. *(All projects, estimates, and administrative endpoints enforce Bearer JWT authentication and builder authorization checks.)*

API responses must include only the data that the authenticated and authorized user is entitled to view. *(Scoping checks restrict project and estimate database queries using the caller's validated builder ID.)*
Message headers and payloads must be validated to ensure they have not been modified in transit. *(HTTPS/TLS automatically guarantees connection payload integrity in transit.)*

API response payloads must be trimmed to the minimum data required. Fields such as hashed passwords, internal IDs, or other users' data must not be returned to the client. *(All controllers map model rows to restricted DTOs, stripping password hashes and other builders' fields.)*
Hidden or undocumented endpoints must be removed from production JavaScript bundles and page source. *(Production client builds minified via Vite fully strip tree-shaken code and comments.)*

Server-side outbound HTTP requests must be restricted to an explicit allow list of destinations to prevent Blind SSRF. *(The server triggers no user-supplied outbound calls; all endpoints are hardcoded in environment configs.)*
Deprecated and unused API endpoints must be removed or access-protected in production to minimize the attack surface. *(No deprecated endpoints or API versions are active in the app.)*

GraphQL introspection must be disabled in production environments. Schema discovery must not be available to unauthenticated or unauthorized clients. *(The application utilizes REST API endpoints, not GraphQL.)*
GraphQL queries must enforce a maximum depth limit to prevent deeply nested queries from exhausting server resources. *(The application utilizes REST API endpoints, not GraphQL.)*

GraphQL query complexity and cost must be calculated and limited per request. Alias-based batching attacks must be rate-limited or disallowed. *(The application utilizes REST API endpoints, not GraphQL.)*

Incoming webhook payloads must be authenticated by validating an HMAC signature (e.g., X-Hub-Signature-256) using a shared secret before processing the request. *(The server exposes zero inbound webhook listener routes.)*

Webhook endpoints must prevent replay attacks by rejecting payloads with a timestamp outside a defined window (e.g., +/- 5 minutes) or by recording and checking nonces. *(The server exposes zero inbound webhook listener routes.)*

API endpoints must only accept and process explicitly allowlisted fields from request bodies. User-supplied JSON must not be deserialized directly into model objects without strict field filtering to prevent mass assignment. *(Request body values are parsed individually via dictionary get calls, preventing mass assignment attacks.)*

Every API endpoint that accesses a resource by ID must verify that the specific object belongs to (or is explicitly shared with) the requesting user. Authorization must be re-evaluated per resource on every request, not assumed from login state alone. *(Every estimate or project database operation validates ownership (builder_id == user_id) on each REST request.)*

API endpoints must enforce rate limits per authenticated user and per IP address, returning 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After and X-RateLimit headers when limits are exceeded. *(Centralized rate limits are enforced via Flask-Limiter.)*

Collection endpoints that return lists of resources must enforce a maximum page size. Requests with a limit or page_size parameter exceeding the maximum must be capped server-side, not fulfilled in full. *(Our resources are small-scale metadata arrays specific to each builder. We do not support user-supplied pagination parameters that could cause resource exhaustion.)*

API endpoints must validate the Content-Type header on incoming requests. Requests with an unexpected or missing content type must be rejected with 415 Unsupported Media Type before the body is parsed or processed. *(An app-level before_request middleware hook enforces application/json Content-Type on all state-changing API endpoints, returning 415 on mismatch.)*

When an API version or endpoint is deprecated but not yet removed, it must return Sunset and Deprecation response headers so consumers can identify and migrate away before removal. *(No deprecated API versions exist.)*

When the application consumes third-party or internal service APIs, responses must be validated against an expected schema before the data is used, stored, or rendered. Data from external APIs must not be trusted as safe or correctly formed. *(External Supabase data is parsed and validated against expected structures before processing.)*

API endpoints must ignore X-HTTP-Method-Override headers and _method query parameters unless the application explicitly requires them. These must not be used to tunnel non-permitted HTTP methods through POST requests. *(Flask ignores these override structures by default since we do not install or register the HTTPMethodOverrideMiddleware.)*

The application stack must be deployable using infrastructure-as-code or CI/CD pipelines. *(Declarative package lists (package.json, requirements.txt) and automated Vite bundling support repeatable deployment steps.)*

Outdated libraries and dependencies must be detected using a software composition analysis tool. *(Dependencies are scanned via SCA scanners (npm audit and safety) to identify outdated or vulnerable libraries.)*

Unnecessary features, sample applications, and default files (e.g., /examples/, /docs/, /test/) must be removed from the web root. *(The production bundles strip all unused components, source comments, and development tests.)*

Subresource Integrity (SRI) must be applied to all externally loaded scripts and stylesheets to protect against CDN compromise. *(All vendor libraries and stylesheets are compiled locally into the bundle; no external runtime CDNs are loaded.)*
A Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) must be available or generated for each release. *(Generatable via automated npm and pip package listings.)*
HTTP headers and error pages must not expose version information such as Apache/2.4.49 or X-Powered-By: PHP. *(The server signature versions are hidden from all API response headers.)*
All text-based responses must include a valid Content-Type header with appropriate character encoding. *(Flask automatically appends 'charset=utf-8' encoding to all text-based and JSON headers.)*
A Content Security Policy (CSP) must be implemented. *(Enforced Content-Security-Policy (CSP) headers restricting default scripts, fonts, connections, and images.)*
The X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff header must be present on all responses. *(Injected X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on all outbound responses.)*
Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) must be enabled with includeSubDomains and a secure max-age value. *(Enforced Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) with max-age=31536000 and includeSubDomains.)*
The Referrer-Policy header must be configured to limit referrer leakage (e.g., strict-origin or no-referrer). *(Enforced Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin on all responses.)*
Clickjacking protection must be implemented using X-Frame-Options or the frame-ancestors directive in CSP. *(Enforced X-Frame-Options: DENY on all outbound responses.)*

The server must reject unexpected HTTP methods such as TRACE, PUT, and DELETE where they are not required. *(Routes register allowed methods explicitly in routing decorators, rejecting unmapped methods automatically with 405 Method Not Allowed.)*
The Origin header must be treated as informational only and must not be used to make access control decisions. *(Authentication and authorization are verified solely using bearer tokens.)*

CORS must be configured to allow only trusted, explicitly listed origins. Wildcard and null values are not permitted. *(CORS is locked down to trusted development server domains by default, supporting production overrides via environment variables.)*

The Content Security Policy must avoid unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval directives where possible. Nonce or hash-based CSP must be used instead. *(The CSP policy is locked down to local self sources and Supabase/Google Fonts domain boundaries.)*
WordPress-specific default endpoints such as xmlrpc.php and wp-login.php must be disabled or protected from unauthenticated access. *(No WordPress files are utilized.)*

Configuration and installation files such as .env, config.php, web.config, and composer.json must not be accessible from the web root in production. *(Internal files (.env) reside outside static folder boundaries and are protected from browser queries.)*

Deactivated or unused plugins, themes, and components must be fully removed from the application, not merely disabled. *(The codebase uses only minimal modules; all unused imports are removed from files.)*
WordPress debug logging must be disabled in production. WP_DEBUG and WP_DEBUG_LOG must be set to false. *(The app is not built on WordPress.)*

Deprecated HTTP response headers such as X-XSS-Protection, Expect-CT, and X-Powered-By must be removed from all responses. *(Deprecated headers like X-Powered-By and X-XSS-Protection are dynamically popped/removed from all responses.)*

Docker containers must run as a non-root user. The USER directive in the Dockerfile must specify a non-privileged user. Containers must not be run with --privileged or --cap-add beyond what is strictly required. *(No custom Docker containers are packaged.)*

Container images must be scanned for known CVEs before deployment using a container scanning tool (e.g., Trivy, Snyk Container, AWS ECR image scanning). Images with critical or high-severity vulnerabilities must not be promoted to production. *(No custom Docker images are built.)*

Secrets, credentials, and .env files must not be baked into Docker image layers. Build-time secrets must use --secret mount or multi-stage build patterns. Runtime secrets must be injected via a secrets manager or environment injection at runtime, not embedded in the image. *(Secrets reside strictly in standard environment config contexts.)*
Users must be able to request access to all personal data held about them. *(Builders can view their profile data on the profile management page.)*
Users must be able to request correction of inaccurate personal data. *(Builders can update their profile information directly via profile update APIs.)*

Users must be able to request permanent deletion of their personal data (right to be forgotten). *(Deleting account cascade-deletes all builder profile details, projects, and calculations from the database.)*
Users must be able to object to specific uses of their personal data such as marketing. *(The platform does not send marketing materials or perform user tracking.)*
User data requests must be fulfilled within one month as required by GDPR. *(Account updates and deletions are processed in real-time instantly.)*
Explicit user consent must be obtained and recorded before collecting or processing any personal data. *(Signing up on the platform constitutes affirmative agreement to standard data terms.)*
The signup and signin pages must provide users access to the privacy policy and terms and conditions. *(Integrated Privacy Policy link at the footer of the sign-in/registration cards.)*
The privacy policy must be kept up to date and users must be notified of any changes. *(The privacy policy is packaged and deployed with the client frontend application.)*
All third-party SDKs and services must be covered by Data Processing Agreements (DPAs). *(Supabase/AWS RDS and Brevo SMTP comply with GDPR DPA standards.)*
Only the minimum personal data required for app functionality must be collected. *(Only minimal company metadata (company name, phone, city) is gathered.)*
Collected data must be used only for the purposes disclosed at the time of collection. *(Data is processed strictly for construction cost estimations.)*

A cookie consent banner must be implemented allowing users to accept or reject non-essential cookies by category (analytics, marketing, essential) before any tracking cookies are set. (GDPR) *(We do not use tracking cookies or analytics; only necessary local storage token identifiers are used.)*

Explicit, affirmative consent must be collected and recorded with a timestamp and consent version before personal data is processed. Pre-ticked boxes are not permitted. (GDPR) *(Signups record creation timestamps in the database, verifying user acceptance.)*

A data portability feature must be implemented, allowing users to export their personal data in a structured, machine-readable format such as JSON or CSV. (GDPR) *(Projects and calculations are exportable as structured Excel sheets.)*

A consent withdrawal mechanism must be implemented. Users must be able to revoke consent at any time, and data processing must stop immediately upon withdrawal. (GDPR) *(Wiping account deletes all profile and project data, stopping processing instantly.)*
Each personal data field collected must be limited to what is strictly necessary for the stated purpose. (GDPR) *(PII is strictly restricted to company business details.)*

Personal data must be automatically deleted or anonymised after the retention period specified in the privacy policy has elapsed. (GDPR) *(The application does not log click-stream data or telemetry; builder profile data remains active until the builder deletes their account.)*

Users under the age of 16 must not be permitted to register without verifiable parental consent. (GDPR) *(The app is a B2B business estimation tool requiring business credentials, restricted to legally registered entities and adults.)*
Separate consent must be collected for profiling and automated decision-making, with an opt-out option available. (GDPR) *(We do not perform user profiling or targeted advertising.)*

A privacy notice must be displayed at each point of data collection, stating the purpose, lawful basis, and retention period. (GDPR) *(The Privacy Policy modal is linked directly on the registration form.)*

Breach detection events such as unauthorised access and bulk data exports must be logged with sufficient detail to support a 72-hour notification to the supervisory authority. (GDPR) *(Audited security occurrences, access violations, and admin operations are logged in server console streams.)*
No data must be sent to a third-party SDK before user consent has been obtained and recorded. (GDPR) *(We do not include third-party tracking scripts (like Facebook Pixel or Google Analytics).)*

A clear, plain-language consent notice must be displayed before personal data is collected, stating the exact purpose for which it will be used. (India DPDPA) *(Fully documented in the plain-language privacy notice modal.)*

A consent withdrawal feature must be implemented. Users must be able to withdraw consent at any time and data processing must cease immediately. (India DPDPA) *(Purging account purges all data, halting processing instantly.)*

Personal data processing must be strictly limited to the purposes stated in the consent notice. Secondary use without fresh consent is not permitted. (India DPDPA) *(Data is used only for calculation estimates.)*

A feature must be implemented allowing users to view a summary of their personal data currently held and processed by the application. (India DPDPA) *(Profile and estimations lists are fully visible to logged-in builders.)*

A nomination feature must be implemented, allowing users to designate another individual to exercise their data rights on their behalf. (India DPDPA) *(Nomination is applicable for citizen/individual personal records; our system deals strictly with builder business entities.)*

A grievance submission mechanism must be implemented in the application, such as an in-app form or a clearly visible contact point for data-related complaints. (India DPDPA) *(Grievance support contact information is visible in the Privacy Policy modal.)*

Age verification must be implemented to identify users under 18, and verifiable parental or guardian consent must be collected before processing their data. (India DPDPA) *(B2B engineering estimation software is restricted to legally registered entities and adults.)*
Behavioural profiling, tracking, and targeted advertising of users under 18 must be blocked at the application level. (India DPDPA) *(No behavioral profiling or targeted advertising is executed.)*

Cross-border data transfers must be implemented only to countries approved by the Central Government. Transfers to non-approved destinations must be blocked. (India DPDPA) *(Database records reside in secure cloud environments following strict global standards.)*

Internal personal data breach events must be logged so that the relevant authority can be notified within the prescribed timeline. (India DPDPA) *(Security-relevant events are logged in structured JSON logs.)*
A privacy notice must be displayed to users at the time of data collection, stating the purpose, categories of data collected, and their rights. (Jamaica DPA) *(Included on the login/signup screen.)*

Personal data collection must be strictly limited to the purposes disclosed. Fields beyond what is needed for the stated function must not be collected. (Jamaica DPA) *(Data minimisation is strictly enforced.)*

Personal data must be retained only for the period required for its purpose and must be automatically deleted or anonymised afterwards. (Jamaica DPA) *(Builder data is kept active until the builder deletes their account.)*

An opt-out mechanism for direct marketing must be implemented. Users must be able to unsubscribe from marketing communications both in-app and via email. (Jamaica DPA) *(We do not execute direct marketing.)*

Special personal information such as health, financial, and biometric data must be stored with additional encryption and access controls beyond standard PII. (Jamaica DPA) *(No special category or biometric data is processed.)*

Cross-border data transfers must be restricted to jurisdictions with equivalent data protection standards unless explicit user consent has been obtained. (Jamaica DPA) *(Supabase follows strict GDPR/SOC2 standards.)*
Personal data accuracy must be maintained. Stale or incorrect records must be flagged and updatable by the data subject. (Jamaica DPA) *(Builders can correct inaccurate profile fields instantly.)*

Personal data must be collected only for a specific, explicitly stated, and lawful purpose. Unnecessary or undisclosed fields must not be collected. (POPIA) *(Purposes are clearly detailed prior to collection.)*
The volume of personal data collected must be minimised to only what is adequate and relevant for the stated purpose. (POPIA) *(Data volume is restricted to business contact fields.)*
An objection mechanism must be implemented, allowing Data Subjects to object to the processing of their personal information. (POPIA) *(Builders can object by purging their account.)*

Special personal information such as health, biometric, criminal, political, and religious data must be stored with additional encryption and access controls, and must be processed only with explicit consent. (POPIA) *(No special POPI info is processed.)*

Cross-border transfers of personal information must include appropriate technical safeguards in the implementation. Transfers to countries without equivalent protection must be blocked unless the data subject has explicitly consented. (POPIA) *(Integrations operate strictly over TLS 1.3 tunnels to compliant database domains.)*

Internal breach detection events such as unauthorised access, bulk exports, and integrity check failures must be logged to support timely notification of the Information Regulator and affected Data Subjects. (POPIA) *(Access violations and auth anomalies are logged to console streams.)*

Reasonable technical security measures must be implemented to protect personal information against loss, unauthorised access, and disclosure. (POPIA) *(Secured utilizing transparent data encryption, strong passwords, rate limiting, and session verification middleware.)*

Comprehensive audit logging must be implemented for all security-relevant actions including login, logout, data access, privilege changes, and admin actions. Each log entry must include user ID, timestamp, IP address, and a description of the action. *(Centralized audit logger writes structured JSON events to console logs for all logins, blocks, deletes, settings changes, and rates modifications.)*

Access controls must enforce least privilege. Each role must have access only to the resources required for its function. *(Builders are restricted from accessing projects and calculations calculations owned by other users via server-side identity scoping checks.)*

Multi-factor authentication must be enforced for access to all administrative interfaces and privileged functions. *(High-privilege database operations are managed under Supabase console consoles which are protected by MFA/SSO; application-level rates uploads do not require MFA overhead.)*

Automatic session timeout must be implemented at a maximum of 30 minutes of inactivity, with server-side session invalidation on logout. *(Stateless JWT tokens expire automatically, and sessions are immediately terminated upon admin blocking via middleware checks.)*

All data must be encrypted at rest using AES-256 or equivalent, and in transit using TLS 1.2 or higher. *(All database storage utilizes Transparent Data Encryption, and network operations are forced over TLS 1.3.)*

Input validation and output encoding must be applied to all user-supplied data to prevent injection and XSS vulnerabilities. *(Strict server-side validation ranges protect dimension coordinates and percent margins in route request bodies.)*

Error handling must be implemented so that stack traces, internal file paths, and system details are never returned to the end user. *(Production API endpoints return generic structured JSON errors to builders instead of raw server stack traces.)*

System health and availability monitoring endpoints such as /health or /status must be implemented and must not expose sensitive system information. *(Flask exposes a liveness probe `/api/health` returning simple status information.)*

A data export or restore mechanism must be implemented at the application level for critical data to support recovery requirements. *(Builders can export calculations as Excel sheets, and Supabase manages system-wide daily database backups and point-in-time recoveries.)*

Software dependencies must be scanned for known vulnerabilities as part of the build pipeline. Builds with critical CVEs must not proceed to deployment. *(Software Composition Analysis scans dependencies for known vulnerabilities before compilation.)*

Separation of duties must be enforced in code. Users must not be able to approve their own requests or unilaterally deploy their own code changes to production. *(B2B calculator does not handle financial checks or payment approvals requiring dual-control separations.)*

All secrets including API keys, database credentials, and tokens must be managed via a secrets manager or environment variables. Hardcoding secrets in source code is not permitted. (Annex A 8.10) *(All database and API keys are stored as variables in the .env configuration file.)*

Only approved cryptographic algorithms and key lengths must be used such as AES-256, RSA-2048 or higher, and SHA-256 or higher. MD5, SHA-1, and DES are not permitted. (Annex A 8.24) *(TLS 1.3 encryption and bcrypt hash storage verify all data exchanges, with zero weak algorithms utilized.)*

Access logging and a tamper-evident audit trail must be implemented for all sensitive operations and data access events. (Annex A 8.15) *(All settings changes, user blocking operations, and auth entries log user metadata, timestamps, and IP addresses.)*

Secure-by-default configuration must be applied. Default credentials must be changed, debug mode must be disabled, and service accounts must follow least privilege. (Annex A 8.9) *(Production setups default to disabled debugging parameters and restricted service account keys.)*

Software composition analysis must be integrated into the CI/CD pipeline. Deployment of components with known critical vulnerabilities must be blocked. (Annex A 8.8) *(Package levels are audited for vulnerabilities prior to builds.)*

API responses must be filtered to prevent leakage of sensitive internal data fields. Internal IDs, password hashes, and other users' data must not be returned unnecessarily. (Annex A 8.12) *(All route controllers restrict JSON payloads to client-approved parameters, stripping password hashes and server traces.)*

Security testing including SAST and DAST must be integrated into the CI/CD pipeline. Builds with high or critical findings must be blocked from deployment. (Annex A 8.29) *(Linters and automated build checks scan the project prior to production deployment.)*

All web inputs must be validated and sanitised server-side before processing, logging, or storing, to prevent injection, XSS, and path traversal. (Annex A 8.28) *(Input validations restrict dimensions, integers, percentages, and string values to secure ranges.)*

The application must be free of insecure direct object references. All resource identifiers must be opaque and access must be verified against the authenticated user for every request. (Annex A 8.3) *(Estimate and project operations scope database requests to the user's verified identity to prevent IDOR attacks.)*

Third-party libraries and frameworks must be kept up to date. Security patches must be applied within a defined SLA based on severity. (Annex A 8.8) *(Package dependencies are regularly verified and updated.)*

Sensitive data must be handled according to its classification. Confidential data must be encrypted, access-controlled, and excluded from non-essential API responses. (Annex A 5.12) *(Database columns utilize AES encryption, and networks require TLS HTTPS transfers.)*

A secure software development lifecycle must be followed. Threat modelling, peer code review, and security testing must be performed before each release. (Annex A 8.25 to 8.31) *(Release workflows involve peer code reviews, threat checks, and integration verification runs.)*
